author: @bearnaff9387
text:
If this were the best kind of sci-fi story, watching all of these videos in order would eventually cause you to develop a consistent and applicable understanding of engineering that is at right-angles to physics as the rest of the world knows it.  Video 2500 would show how to apply all of the principles being illustrated here in order to build an electromechanical device capable of flattening local spacetime, removing the effect of the device's mass and inertia.  The reason for Mr. Murray-Smith's joyful cackling would finally become clear as his legions of feral home tinkerers take to the skies in zero-G inertia-less skycraft.  On that day he reveals his true name to the world, "Doctor Paradigm, Ruler of the Skies and Master of the World!"

Seriously, though, intended or not these videos always feel like they're entries in a series that's building to some purpose.  I just assume it's mad science because of the way our man takes joy in mechanisms working as intended.  I live for this stuff.

author: @drakinfire7205
text:
i had a idea i've not seen this but use the expansion of water as it freezes in a chamber with a piston on top so as the water freezes the piston moves up to generate energy from cold.
water expand 9% of its volume when frozen and exert (24,000 to 114,000 psi) if you convert psi to joules thats 4.686MJ to 22.257MJ ... to "recharge it, you would have to melt the ice"
not sure if it will work right but to use cold to make energy would be nice.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: how about that - i have been pondering this for a while!

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
Sounds like a great idea for colder climates and high deserts where it freezes at night and warms up during the day. 
The way I'd envision it is some device outside has the water and piston, when the water freezes the high torque gets converted into high speed to produce electricity, or if indoors heat production is the end goal, the piston rod could simply be connected to another piston at the other end, to compress a gas to produce heat directly, without mechanical losses other than the piston rings or seal.
Intuitively I don't see compressed gas producing enough heat to warm up a home through the night, but my experience is with 40 to 100 PSI, not 24K+, and nothing prevents scaling up the system until it provides the output desired, so in principle it should be possible.
The tricky part would be to have just the right day/night temperatures to get the liquid/solid phases each day.
In a typically sunny area it would be easier as the sun could warm up the water cylinder regardless of the air temperature.
